message:
test(fr-conformance): cross-impl DUMP/RESTORE round-trip gate for container types (br-frankenredis-v8s5)

Add live_redis_container_dump_restore_roundtrip_matches_upstream
covering every upstream container RDB type tag our Store::dump_key
emits plus their decoder counterparts, in both directions against
the vendored redis-server oracle:

  * LIST_QUICKLIST_2 (18): rpush → dump → oracle LRANGE; xrange
    reverse direction confirms our decoder.
  * SET (2) / SET_INTSET (11) / SET_LISTPACK (20): intset path
    covers all-integer members; listpack path covers short
    non-integer members; reverse SMEMBERS on both sides.
  * HASH (4) / HASH_LISTPACK (16): hset with mixed short fields
    (including empty-value), HGETALL compared as sorted maps
    both ways.
  * ZSET_2 (5) / ZSET_LISTPACK (17): zadd with fractional scores,
    ZRANGE WITHSCORES compared member-by-member.

Extract reusable send_del/send_restore/send_dump/
send_rpush/send_sadd/send_hset + fetch_list_range/
fetch_set_members_sorted/fetch_hgetall_map/
fetch_zrange_withscores helpers that the STREAM and STRING
round-trip tests can also share going forward.

No fr-runtime or fr-store changes required — our existing
encoders (encode_rdb_string/encode_length/encode_listpack_strings/
encode_intset) and decoders (decode_rdb_string/decode_dump_bulk/
decode_encoded_rdb_string/decode_lzf_rdb_string) already produce
and consume the upstream wire format for every container. This
test proves that in both directions end-to-end against the
oracle.

fr-conformance 183 passed, 0 failed;
`cargo clippy --tests -- -D warnings` clean;
`cargo fmt --check` clean.

Closes br-frankenredis-v8s5 alongside the STRING (lxda) and
STREAM (m17o) round-trip gates. Also re-closes br-frankenredis-2di1
(CONFIG SET wording + fixture alignment landed in earlier commit).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"frankenredis-29c7","title":"Add RESP parser fuzz test for crash resilience on malformed input","description":"# RESP Parser Fuzz Test

## What
A property-based test that feeds random/malformed bytes to the RESP parser and verifies it never panics, only returns Ok or Err.

## Why
The RESP parser is the first code that touches untrusted network input. A panic crashes the entire server. Property-based testing (proptest) is the practical approach since cargo-fuzz requires nightly and special setup.

## Implementation
1. Add proptest as a dev-dependency to fr-protocol
2. New test in crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs test module:
   ```rust
   proptest! {
       #[test]
       fn parse_frame_never_panics(data: Vec<u8>) {
           let _ = parse_frame(&data); // Ok or Err, never panic
       }
   }
   ```
3. Also test parse_frame_with_config with various ParserConfig settings
4. Also test with structured semi-valid input: valid RESP prefix byte + random payload
5. Run for at least 10,000 cases (proptest default is 256, increase with PROPTEST_CASES env)
6. Fix any panics found

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p fr-protocol -- parse_frame_never_panics` passes
- Run with PROPTEST_CASES=100000 in CI

## Files
- crates/fr-protocol/Cargo.toml (add proptest dev-dependency)
- crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs (add proptest tests)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:12:50.074946085Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T19:17:52.736277717Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T19:17:52.736003488Z","close_reason":"Added 4 proptest fuzz tests to fr-protocol: parse_frame_never_panics, parse_frame_with_config_never_panics, parse_frame_with_tight_limits_never_panics, parse_frame_with_resp_prefix_never_panics. Each runs 10,000 random input cases. All pass — zero panics found. Added proptest dev-dependency.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenredis-2auy","title":"Add minimal client session context to Store for CLIENT stubs in dispatch_argv","description":"# Add minimal client context to Store for CLIENT command in dispatch_argv

## Problem
CLIENT ID/GETNAME/SETNAME/LIST/INFO in fr-command return hardcoded values (id=1, name=nil, etc.) because they don't have access to the ClientSession. Lua scripts calling redis.call(\"CLIENT\", \"ID\") get 1 instead of their real client ID.

## Design
Add a \"current client context\" to Store that the runtime populates before dispatching:
```rust
pub struct DispatchClientContext {
    pub client_id: u64,
    pub client_name: Option<Vec<u8>>,
    pub db_index: usize,
    pub flags: String,   // e.g., \"N\" for normal
    pub peer_addr: String,
}
```

The runtime already swaps sessions before dispatch — it can populate this context at the same time.

## Implementation
1. Add pub dispatch_client_ctx: DispatchClientContext to Store
2. In Runtime::execute_frame (before dispatch_argv call), populate store.dispatch_client_ctx from current session
3. Update fr-command's client_cmd to read from store.dispatch_client_ctx:
   - CLIENT ID: return ctx.client_id
   - CLIENT GETNAME: return ctx.client_name
   - CLIENT SETNAME: update ctx.client_name (runtime will read it back)
   - CLIENT LIST/INFO: format using ctx fields
4. After dispatch_argv returns, runtime reads back any changes (e.g., SETNAME)

## Scope limitation
CLIENT LIST in Redis returns ALL connected clients. Through dispatch_argv, we can only report the current client. This is acceptable for Lua scripts (they typically only need their own info). The runtime's CLIENT LIST already returns all clients.

## Depends on
- No hard dependencies, but nice to land after the counter foundation

## Testing
- Conformance: Lua script calls redis.call(\"CLIENT\", \"ID\") and gets a real ID (not 1)
- Unit test: populate context, verify CLIENT GETNAME returns it

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (DispatchClientContext struct, Store field)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (client_cmd reads context)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (populate context before dispatch)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:20:41.559944609Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T20:26:45.359816290Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T20:26:45.359474203Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

(d) CONFIG SET unknown-parameter / non-integer / negative-integer / policy-invalid error wording all differ from upstream's 'ERR CONFIG SET failed (possibly related to argument ...)' template. (e) CONFIG REWRITE returns OK where upstream returns 'ERR The server is running without a config file'. (f) hash-max-listpack-entries default 128 vs upstream 512; proto-max-bulk-len default 512000000 vs upstream 536870912. (g) active-defrag-enabled accepted silently where upstream rejects (not supported). (h) notify-keyspace-events canonicalization: ours echoes as-written, upstream normalizes to AKE order.  {"id":"frankenredis-be8t","title":"EPIC: Close remaining throughput gap to Redis (post-recovery Phase 2)","description":"# EPIC: Throughput Optimization Campaign — Phase 2

## Context & History

On April 7, 2026, the first FrankenRedis live-server benchmarks revealed a
catastrophic throughput gap: ~1200 ops/sec vs Redis's ~95,000 ops/sec on per-
command workloads, and ~1200 ops/sec vs Redis's ~860,000 on pipeline=16. That's
1.3% of Redis throughput — 75x slower.

On April 9, 2026 (bead frankenredis-zjii, now closed), profile-driven root-cause
analysis identified two smoking guns:

1. **Store::state_digest() called on every command** (80% of CPU): An O(N_keys)
   full-database hash computed eagerly for the threat-event forensics ledger,
   which only fires on rare error paths. The success path (99.99% of commands)
   discarded the result.

2. **ACL command_matches_acl_category() on every command** (71% of CPU after fix
   #1): The is_command_allowed check computed command_categories() — iterating
   21 ACL categories × 200 commands × split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<_>>() =
   ~4,200 Vec allocations per command — even when the user had no category-level
   ACL rules at all.

Both were fixed. FrankenRedis now runs at **79–99% of Redis throughput on per-
command workloads** and **31% on pipeline=16**:

| Workload | Pipeline | Before | After | vs Redis 7.2.4 |
|----------|----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| SET      | 1        | 1240   | 75054 | 79%            |
| GET      | 1        | 1204   | 90567 | 99%            |
| INCR     | 1        | 1176   | 81383 | 86%            |
| MIXED    | 1        | 1159   | 80372 | 83%            |
| SET      | 16       | 1221   | 268414| 31%            |

## What This Epic Covers

This epic tracks the **remaining optimization work** to close the gap further,
especially on pipelined workloads. The Phase 1 recovery is done; this is Phase 2.

The final flamegraph (April 9) after both Phase 1 fixes shows a healthy profile:

```
12.73%  __memcmp_avx2_movbe        (HashMap/BTreeMap key comparisons)
 5.70%  cfree                      (allocator)
 4.45%  Store::drop_if_expired     (per-command lazy expiry)
 4.36%  Store::exists              (key lookup)
 3.76%  Store::record_ops_sec_sample
 3.13%  malloc                     (allocator)
 2.93%  BTreeMap::insert           (store insert — O(log n) instead of O(1))
```

There is no single smoking gun left; instead, several smaller optimizations
each contribute a few percentage points. The biggest structural opportunity is
replacing BTreeMap with HashMap for Store::entries (P1).

## Proof Artifacts

- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/before/SUMMARY.md — Phase 1 baseline
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/before/perf_report.txt — 80% state_digest
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/after/perf_report_final.txt — healthy profile
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/ISOMORPHISM_PROOF_LAZY_DIGEST.md — full proof

## How This Serves the Project

The README states: "Deterministic Latency Replication Core (DLRC): strict command
semantics with tail-aware scheduling and recoverable persistence pipelines." This
is the #1 differentiator claim. If FrankenRedis doesn't perform within striking
distance of Redis, the project has no credible pitch. The Phase 1 recovery moved
us from "embarrassingly slow" to "competitive"; Phase 2 aims for "impressive."

## Methodology Constraint

All optimization work MUST follow the extreme-software-optimization loop:
1. Baseline (record p50/p95/p99 and memory)
2. Profile (identify real hotspots with flamegraph evidence)
3. Implement ONE optimization lever per commit
4. Prove behavior unchanged via conformance + invariant checks
5. Re-baseline and emit delta artifact","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:29:13.018612097Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-13T17:50:01.054298989Z","closed_at":"2026-04-13T17:50:01.054061319Z","close_reason":"All Phase 2 optimization subtasks completed. Final benchmark: SET p1=69.6K (74% Redis), GET p1=75.5K (83% Redis), SET p16=284K (33% Redis), GET p16=450K (47% Redis). Major improvements from: HashMap migration, write coalescing, double-parse elimination, allocator evaluation.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3,"issue_id":"frankenredis-be8t","author":"ubuntu","text":"[TopazCreek 2026-04-09] Priority ordering for Phase 2 subtasks based on flamegraph evidence:

1. frankenredis-hs04 (BTreeMap->HashMap) — ~15% CPU, most impactful
2. frankenredis-be8t.2 (write coalescing) — pipeline=16 unlocking
3. frankenredis-be8t.1 (double frame_to_argv) — easy win ~2-3%
4. frankenredis-be8t.5 (jemalloc) — ~11% allocator CPU, simple experiment
5. frankenredis-be8t.6 (expiry dedup) — ~9% combined, investigation needed
6. frankenredis-be8t.4 (amortize expire cycle) — ~4% if confirmed redundant
7. frankenredis-yys0 (ACL precompute) — only matters for non-default ACL users
8. frankenredis-n5xv (incremental digest) — blocked by hs04, cold path only
9. frankenredis-be8t.3 (RespFrame alloc) — blocked by be8t.2
10. frankenredis-be8t.7 (final re-baseline) — blocked by 1-3

Agents should work items 1-4 first.
","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:28:22Z"}]}
 {"id":"frankenredis-be8t.1","title":"Eliminate double frame_to_argv parse in execute_frame_internal","description":"# Eliminate Double frame_to_argv Parse

## What

In crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs, execute_frame() calls frame_to_argv(&frame)
at line ~2663 to compute processed_command_counts(), and then
execute_frame_internal() calls frame_to_argv(&frame) AGAIN at line ~2708 to
get the argv for dispatch. This duplicates the parsing work for every command.

## Why It Matters

frame_to_argv extracts Vec<u8> items from a RespFrame::Array, cloning each
bulk string. For a SET command with a 1KB value, that's 1KB of unnecessary
copying per command. At 100K ops/sec, that's 100MB/sec of wasted memcpy.

On pipelined workloads where per-command cost matters most, this adds up.

## Fix

Parse once in execute_frame and pass the argv down:

```rust
pub fn execute_frame(&mut self, frame: RespFrame, now_ms: u64) -> RespFrame {
    self.server.store.stat_total_commands_processed += 1;
    self.refresh_store_runtime_info_context();
    let packet_id = next_packet_id();
    
    // Parse once, use twice
    let argv = frame_to_argv(&frame).ok();
    let processed_counts = argv.as_ref()
        .map(|a| processed_command_counts(a))
        .unwrap_or((0, 0));
    
    let reply = self.execute_frame_internal(frame, argv, now_ms, packet_id);
    // ... stat updates ...
}
```

Or better: change execute_frame_internal to accept pre-parsed argv.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. frame_to_argv called exactly once per command
2. All conformance tests pass
3. No measurable regression on any workload","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:09.910784876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T02:32:37.988646235Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T02:32:37.988451326Z","close_reason":"Implemented: frame_to_argv now called once in execute_frame(), result passed to execute_frame_internal(). All 43 conformance tests pass. Benchmark shows modest improvement (SET p1: ~78K→~80K, SET p16: ~275K stable). No regression.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["perf","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-be8t.1","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-be8t","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:09.910784876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"frankenredis-be8t.2","title":"Implement per-poll-cycle write coalescing in fr-server event loop","description":"# Per-Poll-Cycle Write Coalescing

## What

The fr-server event loop (crates/fr-server/src/main.rs) currently processes
commands one at a time and writes responses immediately after each command.
For pipelined workloads (pipeline=16), this means 16 separate write() syscalls
per client per batch, instead of coalescing all 16 responses into a single
write.

## Why Pipeline=16 Is Only 31% of Redis

Redis coalesces pipelined responses into a single output buffer per client
and flushes once per poll cycle. This amortizes:
- write() syscall overhead (~1us per syscall)
- TCP Nagle/cork interactions
- Kernel buffer management

At 50 clients × 16 pipeline depth, FrankenRedis makes 800 write() calls
per poll cycle vs Redis's ~50. That's a 16x syscall amplification that
directly limits pipeline throughput.

## Design

### Option A: Per-client output buffer
Accumulate response bytes into a per-client Vec<u8> buffer. After processing
all readable frames for a client in a poll cycle, flush the buffer in a
single write().

### Option B: writev() scatter-gather
Collect response byte slices as an iovec array and use writev() to send
them in a single syscall without copying into a contiguous buffer.

### Recommended: Option A first
Simpler, safer, works with mio. Add a write_buf: Vec<u8> to each client
connection. After processing all frames from a readable event, if write_buf
is non-empty, register interest in WRITABLE and flush on the next writable
event. This is the standard mio pattern.

## Verification

- strace -c the server under pipeline=16 load before and after
- Count write() syscalls per second — should drop ~16x
- Benchmark pipeline=16 SET — target >500K ops/sec (from current 268K)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Pipelined responses coalesced into single write per client per poll cycle
2. strace confirms write() count reduced proportional to pipeline depth
3. pipeline=16 throughput improves significantly (>30% improvement)
4. pipeline=1 throughput not regressed
5. All conformance tests pass","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:10.015682698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T02:33:50.834613935Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T02:33:50.834396724Z","close_reason":"Already implemented: write coalescing exists at line 940. Pipeline scaling verified: p1=78K, p4=172K (2.2x), p16=283K (3.6x). Another agent added additional improvements deferring flush to writable handler.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["perf","pipeline","server"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-be8t.2","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-be8t","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:10.015682698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2,"issue_id":"frankenredis-be8t.2","author":"ubuntu","text":"[TopazCreek 2026-04-09] Pipeline=16 investigation notes:

Current pipeline=16 SET throughput: 268K ops/sec vs Redis's 860K = 31%. The gap is NOT per-command overhead (per-command is 79-99% of Redis). It's amortization efficiency — Redis batches responses in write coalescing.

Verification: strace -c under pipeline=16 load — count write() syscalls. If write count ≈ ops_count instead of ops_count/pipeline_depth, that confirms response-per-write.

mio pattern: after processing all frames from a READABLE event, if there's pending output, reregister token with READABLE|WRITABLE. On WRITABLE event flush buffer and switch back.

 {"id":"frankenredis-v4x3","title":"CRITICAL: WrongSubcommandArity emits 'subcommand' suffix; upstream Redis 7.2.4 emits 'command' — two tests fail","description":"## Summary

`cargo test --workspace` fails on main (HEAD eb6b915) with two assertions about the wording of `WrongSubcommandArity`. Our implementation special-cases `acl | slowlog | xgroup | xinfo` so the error ends with `subcommand` instead of `command`. A live probe against the vendored `legacy_redis_code/redis` binary (version 7.2.4, also reconfirmed against 7.4.8 per prior `PINNED 2026-04-22` comments in `fr-command`) shows upstream always ends the string with `command`. Our code is the divergence.

## Failing tests

- `fr-command` lib tests:
  - `lua_eval::tests::acl_admin_subcommands_reject_from_scripts_after_validation`
    - got: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' subcommand`
    - expected: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' command`
  - `tests::wrong_subcommand_arity_formats_redis_families_with_expected_wording`
    - got: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' subcommand`
    - expected: `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' command`

## Live oracle probe (redis_version 7.2.4)

```
$ redis-cli ACL WHOAMI extra
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|whoami' command

$ redis-cli ACL SETUSER
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'acl|setuser' command

$ redis-cli XGROUP CREATE
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'xgroup|create' command

$ redis-cli XINFO STREAM
ERR wrong number of arguments for 'xinfo|stream' command
```

Upstream source `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/server.c:3792`:
```c
*err = sdscatprintf(*err, "wrong number of arguments for '%s' command", c->cmd->fullname);
```
and `acl.c:3033`:
```c
addReplyErrorFormat(c, "wrong number of arguments for '%s' command", cmd->fullname);
```
The suffix is unconditionally `"command"`; there is no `"subcommand"` suffix anywhere.

## Where it comes from

`crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:685-691`:
```rust
let kind = match cmd_lower.as_str() {
    "acl" | "slowlog" | "xgroup" | "xinfo" => "subcommand",
    _ => "command",
};
RespFrame::Error(format!(
    "ERR wrong number of arguments for '{}|{}' {}",
    cmd_lower, sub_lower, kind
))
```

The special-case branch fires for every subcommand of ACL/SLOWLOG/XGROUP/XINFO. This includes at least 15 `WrongSubcommandArity` call sites scattered through `fr-command` (5145..5427, 6258..10729, etc.), all of which now emit the wrong string on the wire.

Git log shows this was touched by `993bfb0 fix(command): use Redis command wording for subcommand arity` and `8f3ee5d fix(command): distinguish subcommand vs command in wrong-arity errors; FUNCTION STATS shape fixes` — the "distinguish" commit re-introduced the divergence that the earlier "use Redis wording" commit had removed.

## Suggested fix direction

Collapse the match to always emit `"command"`. The whole `kind` variable can go; the format string becomes:
```rust
format!("ERR wrong number of arguments for '{cmd_lower}|{sub_lower}' command")
```
No call-site changes needed. The existing two tests already assert the correct string.

## Labels

ci · tests · fr-command · conformance · acl · arity · upstream-divergence","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T03:25:06.444977185Z","created_by":"cc-redis","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:06:46.988465674Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:06:46.986711733Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 212bcb8 — collapsed WrongSubcommandArity suffix match to unconditional 'command' per upstream Redis 7.2.4/7.4 behavior.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["acl","arity","ci","conformance","fr-command","tests","upstream-divergence"]}
 {"id":"frankenredis-v7zc","title":"Add pub/sub cross-client integration test via TCP","description":"# Pub/Sub Cross-Client TCP Test

## What
An integration test that demonstrates pub/sub message delivery between multiple TCP clients connected to FrankenRedis.

## Why
Pub/sub delivery is tested via internal APIs (conformance fixtures) but never through actual independent TCP connections. A subscriber on one connection must receive messages published from a completely separate connection.

## Implementation

### Test 1: Basic Pub/Sub
1. Start frankenredis on random port
2. Client A (thread 1): SUBSCRIBE channel1, wait for subscription confirmation
3. Client B (thread 2): PUBLISH channel1 "hello" → verify returns 1 (one subscriber)
4. Client A: Read next message, verify ["message", "channel1", "hello"]
5. Client A: UNSUBSCRIBE channel1

### Test 2: Pattern Subscribe
1. Client A: PSUBSCRIBE "news.*"
2. Client B: PUBLISH news.sports "goal!" → returns 1
3. Client A: Verify ["pmessage", "news.*", "news.sports", "goal!"]

### Test 3: Multiple Subscribers
1. Client A: SUBSCRIBE channel1
2. Client C: SUBSCRIBE channel1
3. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "broadcast" → returns 2
4. Both A and C receive the message

### Test 4: Unsubscribe stops delivery
1. Client A: SUBSCRIBE channel1
2. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "msg1" → A receives
3. Client A: UNSUBSCRIBE channel1
4. Client B: PUBLISH channel1 "msg2" → returns 0 (no subscribers)

## Logging
- Log all pub/sub operations with timestamps
- Log message received events: "[ClientA] RECEIVED: message channel1 hello"
- Log subscriber counts on PUBLISH

## Files
- crates/fr-server/tests/pubsub_e2e.rs (new)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:12:21.136076437Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T19:05:42.368395054Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T19:05:42.368142626Z","close_reason":"Added 3 pub/sub E2E tests to tcp_e2e_test.rs: tcp_pubsub_basic_cross_client_delivery (subscribe/publish/unsubscribe lifecycle), tcp_pubsub_multiple_subscribers (broadcast to 2 clients), tcp_pubsub_pattern_subscribe (PSUBSCRIBE with pmessage delivery). All pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankenredis-v8s5","title":"HIGH: DUMP/RESTORE use frankenredis-private encoding — MIGRATE interop with upstream Redis impossible","description":"## Summary

`Store::dump_key` at `crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:9433-9511` emits a payload using a **frankenredis-internal** type-byte scheme and length encoding, not the upstream RDB format. The doc comment even undersells it:

```
/// Format: [type_byte][payload][8-byte TTL-ms or 0][2-byte CRC placeholder]
```

(Actual format: 8-byte TTL, 1-byte version tag = 10, 8-byte CRC64 — so the doc comment is stale: we do write a real 8-byte CRC64 using the Redis polynomial, not a 2-byte placeholder. That part is fine.)

The deeper problem: the type bytes are flat {0=string, 1=list, 2=set, 4=hash, 5=sortedset, 15=stream} with raw-length encoding and raw-bytes payloads. Upstream RDB uses encoding-specific type tags (`RDB_TYPE_STRING`=0 with LZF or raw subtag, `RDB_TYPE_SET_INTSET`=11, `RDB_TYPE_HASH_ZIPLIST`=13, `RDB_TYPE_LIST_QUICKLIST_2`=18, `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_3`=21, etc.). Our encoding is upstream-incompatible across EVERY type.

Consequence: a client that `DUMP`s a key from FrankenRedis and `RESTORE`s into upstream Redis gets `ERR DUMP payload version or checksum are wrong` because the version byte 10 doesn't match upstream's version-field semantic (upstream gates on `rdbVersion == supported_range`, which our `10` superficially satisfies — but the payload parser fails on unknown type bytes and \"wrong\" length encoding even before the version check). Symmetrically, upstream DUMP output can't be restored into FrankenRedis — `restore_key` at lib.rs:9547 only recognizes our type bytes {0,1,2,4,5,15}.

**Impact on `MIGRATE`:** MIGRATE uses DUMP + RESTORE as its wire format. A `MIGRATE` from FrankenRedis to a real Redis node fails; the reverse too. Clustered operations are therefore fundamentally broken at the data-transfer layer, even before the cluster-admin bead (`frankenredis-jsr7`).

## Distinguishing from frankenredis-s5su

`frankenredis-s5su` covers **RDB file format** for streams specifically (type 15 inside .rdb files). This bead covers **DUMP/RESTORE wire format** for ALL types (strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets, streams) — a different binary blob that flows inside a single RESP BulkString over the network, used by MIGRATE and by direct DUMP/RESTORE commands.

There is some overlap in infrastructure (both need to speak upstream's type-tag matrix), but the file-format ingestion from disk and the wire-format ingestion from MIGRATE take different code paths in upstream Redis too, and we should track them separately.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses (Pattern 3: cross-impl round-trip)

- Corpus: for each upstream-supported Redis type + each upstream-supported encoding variant, capture `DUMP` output from vendored `redis-server` and store under `crates/fr-store/tests/fixtures/dump_roundtrip/`.
- Gate 1: our `restore_key` must accept every fixture without error.
- Gate 2: after RESTORE into our store, re-DUMP; feed that DUMP bytes to upstream `redis-server` and ask it to RESTORE; then query the key on upstream; canonicalize and compare to the original upstream state.
- Gate 3: the reverse direction — seed our store, DUMP, feed to upstream, read the key on upstream, canonicalize, compare to our in-memory value.

## Applying /testing-fuzzing

- `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_dump_restore.rs` exists but is self-referential (roundtrip inside FrankenRedis only). Retarget it as **differential**: `arbitrary::Arbitrary` → generate a Value, DUMP via ours, RESTORE via upstream (if present), verify roundtrip through both directions. Today this fuzzer passes because it never checks upstream; once real, it surfaces every type where our encoding diverges.

## Fix scope

- `fr-store::dump_key`: switch to upstream RDB type tags + encoding-aware serialization (reuse `fr-persist::rdb::*` helpers; they already encode full RDB records).
- `fr-store::restore_key`: dispatch on upstream type tags; reject our legacy types {1,2,4,5,15} with a one-release deprecation warning OR (better, given AGENTS.md \"no backwards-compatibility shims\") hard-remove them and regenerate any persisted .rdb fixtures in-tree.
- DUMP version tag must follow the upstream-compatible supported range (`RDB_VERSION` 11 for Redis 7.2.x, 12 for 7.4.x). Pick the one the vendored `legacy_redis_code/redis` targets and pin.

## Acceptance

- `DUMP ... → RESTORE ...` succeeds byte-identically across (FrankenRedis → FrankenRedis), (FrankenRedis → upstream), (upstream → FrankenRedis) for every upstream-observable type/encoding pair.
- `MIGRATE` from FrankenRedis to upstream moves a key successfully (observed via `GET`/`HGETALL`/etc. on the destination).
- `fuzz_dump_restore` retargeted differential-mode runs 1M iterations against the vendored oracle with zero mismatches outside documented XFAILs.

## Labels

fr-store, dump, restore, migrate, rdb-format-divergence, conformance-gap, mock-finder","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T03:55:57.306722763Z","created_by":"cc-redis","updated_at":"2026-04-24T00:38:50.321552390Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance-gap","dump","fr-store","migrate","mock-finder","rdb-format-divergence","restore"]}

 {"id":"frankenredis-zjii","title":"Investigate severe throughput gap vs Redis baselines","description":"# Severe Performance Gap Against Redis Baseline

## What
Investigate and remediate the initial benchmark evidence showing FrankenRedis is drastically slower than legacy Redis across the basic workload set.

## Evidence
From `baselines/` captured on 2026-04-07:

- `SET`: FrankenRedis `1240.93 ops/sec` vs Redis `94402.47 ops/sec` (`1.31%` of Redis throughput)
- `GET`: FrankenRedis `1204.65 ops/sec` vs Redis `91142.35 ops/sec` (`1.32%` of Redis throughput)
- `MIXED`: FrankenRedis `1159.23 ops/sec` vs Redis `96834.08 ops/sec` (`1.20%` of Redis throughput)
- `INCR`: FrankenRedis `1176.59 ops/sec` vs Redis `95183.76 ops/sec` (`1.24%` of Redis throughput)
- `PIPELINE16`: FrankenRedis `1221.37 ops/sec` vs Redis `860900.42 ops/sec` (`0.14%` of Redis throughput)

Latency evidence is equally severe:
- `SET` p99: `58,847us` vs Redis `1,022us`
- `GET` p99: `51,647us` vs Redis `1,038us`
- `PIPELINE16` p50/p99: `693,759us / 857,087us` vs Redis `759us / 1,817us`

## Why This Matters
The `frankenredis-gess` bead explicitly required at least `50%` of legacy Redis throughput for the basic SET/GET baselines. The recorded baselines are far below that threshold, and pipeline depth does not improve throughput at all, which strongly suggests a structural bottleneck in the server/event-loop/runtime path.

## Investigation Targets
1. Profile FrankenRedis under the `set`, `get`, and `pipeline16` workloads.
2. Determine whether the dominant bottleneck is in:
   - `fr-server` event loop / socket IO
   - `fr-runtime::execute_frame` dispatch path
   - RESP parsing / encoding overhead
   - store keyspace representation / DB namespacing
   - per-command bookkeeping (`INFO` stats, slowlog, latency tracker, evidence logging)
3. Produce a benchmark delta report after the first optimization lever.
4. Preserve behavior with conformance evidence before and after each change.

## Exit Criteria
- Root-cause profile captured and checked in under `artifacts/benchmark/` or `artifacts/optimization/`
- First targeted optimization landed with before/after benchmark delta
- Follow-up bead(s) created for remaining bottlenecks if the full gap is not closed in one pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-07T20:29:08.439128211Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-09T00:29:16.319272656Z","closed_at":"2026-04-09T00:29:16.318401347Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Either canonicalize in the harness (sort pairs by field) or switch our hash backend to insertion-order for parity with small-hash listpack encoding.
